title: `tracemalloc` aborts when run from threads in no-gil
author: devdanzin
body:
# Crash report

### What happened?

It's possible to get two different aborts from failed assertions, only with `PYTHON_GIL=0`, using the code below:

```python
from threading import Thread
import _tracemalloc

alive = [
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=(
        (memoryview(bytearray(b"abc\xe9\xff")),))),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=()),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.get_traceback_limit, args=()),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=()),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.stop, args=()),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.reset_peak, args=()),
    Thread(target=_tracemalloc.get_tracemalloc_memory, args=())
]

for obj in alive:
    print('START', obj)
    try:
        obj.start()
    except Exception:
        pass
```

The errors are:
```
python: Python/tracemalloc.c:445: tracemalloc_remove_trace: Assertion `tracemalloc_config.tracing' failed.
Aborted
```
And:
```
python: Python/tracemalloc.c:469: tracemalloc_add_trace: Assertion `tracemalloc_config.tracing' failed.
Aborted
```
Found with fusil by @vstinner.

### CPython versions tested on:

CPython main branch

### Operating systems tested on:

Linux

### Output from running 'python -VV' on the command line:

Python 3.14.0a1+ experimental free-threading build (heads/main:d467d9246c, Nov  1 2024, 09:05:56) [GCC 11.4.0]

comments.author: rruuaanng
comments.body: Maybe we can add `HEAD lock` to `tracemalloc_config.tracing`. This is most likely caused by concurrent operations on a global without locking. If possible, I'll make a PR.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body: Cannot repro anymore, please re-open if you can.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
Oops, maybe spoke too soon: it segfaults on a release build if we create more threads and try more times!

```python
from threading import Thread
import _tracemalloc

for x in range(20):
    alive = []
    for x in range(500):
        alive += [
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=(
                (1,))),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=()),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.get_traceback_limit, args=()),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.start, args=()),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.stop, args=()),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.reset_peak, args=()),
            Thread(target=_tracemalloc.get_tracemalloc_memory, args=())
        ]

    for obj in alive:
        print('START', obj)
        try:
            obj.start()
        except Exception:
            pass
```

comments.author: kumaraditya303
comments.body:
I looked into the thread safety of tracemalloc and there are a few thread safety issues here:

- It changes the reftracer for all threads without any synchronization and leads to data race in `PyRefTracer_SetTracer`.
- It changes the allocator used by runtime while other threads could be allocating memory so it leads to data race in `PyMem_SetAllocator`.
- It creates a new reference to filename which causes issues when used under sub-interpreters https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/134604.

Data races:
<details>
